title: Andrew Carnegie: Gospel of Wealth, Narrated by Brian Cox
text:
Of the many great fortunes built in this
world, one stands out.
In 1901, Andrew Kanegi sold his steel
mills for nearly $500 million, making
him one of the world's richest men.
Today, he is famous not for his fortune,
but for what he did with it. Kanei
believed that surplus wealth came with a
responsibility to help others. So he
pledged to give it all away.
In his gospel of wealth, Kiggi wrote,
>> "The man who dies great dies."
>> At the time, his philosophy was
unprecedented.
Instead of giving handouts, Kanei
offered a hand upward. He invested in
free public libraries, universities,
scientific research, and institutions
for peace.
Above all, Kanei believed in education,
the source of opportunity that allows
people to rise,
to try and make the world in some way
better than you found it. According to
Kanei, is to have a noble motive in
life. His gospel laid the foundation for
modern philanthropy and his conviction
that wealth must serve the community may
be his greatest gift.
